title.en: Piṅgala's Chandaḥśāstra — binary enumeration of meters

dating.display: c. 3rd–2nd century BCE (text); key expansions in later commentary
dating.basis: Dated by language and by position in the śāstra tradition; estimates range from 4th to 2nd century BCE. Some algorithms are explicit only in Halāyudha's 10th-century CE commentary.

positions.holders: Most historians of Indian mathematics (Plofker, Sridharan)

positions.position: Earlier (4th c. BCE, contemporary with or before Pāṇini)
positions.holders: Parts of the Indian tradition identify Piṅgala as Pāṇini's brother

summary: A treatise on Sanskrit prosody that solves a combinatorial problem: enumerate all meters of n syllables, each syllable light (laghu) or heavy (guru). Piṅgala's pratyaya algorithms generate the k-th pattern from its index and recover the index from a pattern — binary encoding and decoding. The mātrā-meter counts (made explicit by Virahāṅka, c. 700 CE) are the Fibonacci numbers; the meru-prastāra laid out by Halāyudha is Pascal's triangle.
whyItMatters: This is the earliest known systematic use of a two-symbol positional enumeration — the combinatorial core of binary representation — done for poetry. It is also the standard example of how Indian mathematics grew inside linguistic sciences rather than geometry.
honestyNote: Piṅgala's own sūtras are terse to the point of cryptic; several celebrated results are explicit only in commentaries up to 1,200 years later. And there is no causal chain from Piṅgala to Leibniz or to digital electronics — the through-line below is anticipation, not transmission. Claims that 'computers run on Piṅgala's binary' belong in the ledger (category 9).

chain: Chandaḥśāstra ch. 8: algorithms mapping meter patterns to indices and back (binary ranking/unranking)
Virahāṅka (c. 700 CE): mātrā-meter counts — the Fibonacci sequence, ~500 years before Fibonacci
Halāyudha (10th c. CE): meru-prastāra — the binomial-coefficient triangle, ~700 years before Pascal
Modern credit: Knuth documents Piṅgala's priority in The Art of Computer Programming, Vol. 4A

sources.label: Van Nooten, B., "Binary Numbers in Indian Antiquity", Journal of Indian Philosophy 21 (1993)
Singh, Parmanand, "The So-called Fibonacci Numbers in Ancient and Medieval India", Historia Mathematica 12 (1985)
Knuth, D., The Art of Computer Programming, Vol. 4A (2011), §7.2.1.7 history notes
Van Nooten, B., Journal of Indian Philosophy 21 (1993)
Plofker, K., Mathematics in India (Princeton, 2009), ch. 3
Sridharan, R., "Sanskrit Prosody, Piṅgala Sūtras and Binary Arithmetic", in Contributions to the History of Indian Mathematics (2005)

dharmicTie.statement: The Chandaḥśāstra IS the chandas vedāṅga — the limb of Vedic study that measures the meters of śruti. The binary enumeration exists because the Veda's poetry needed cataloguing.

scriptureRefs.text: chandaḥ pādau tu vedasya — 'meter is the Veda's two feet'
